steps.title: Resolve the apparent conflict
steps.content: The passage gives two facts that seem difficult to combine. Marginal notes indicate that much of Cantares Mexicanos predates the Spanish invasion, so the collection preserves older Nahuatl poetry. At the same time, some poems contain inarguable references to beliefs and customs common in Spain during that era. The conclusion after Thus must explain how both facts can be true.

steps.title: Eliminate unsupported conclusions
steps.content: Choice B shifts to whether the compilers knew Spanish, but language knowledge does not explain why Spanish beliefs and customs appear in the poems. Choice C introduces borrowing from other societies, which the passage never mentions. Choice D treats the Spanish references as coincidence, but the passage calls those references inarguable, so that explanation conflicts with the evidence.

steps.title: Choose the balanced explanation
steps.content: Choice A keeps both sides of the evidence intact: much of the collection can predate the invasion, while some material can have been added afterward. Later additions would account for the Spanish references without denying that the collection largely preserves pre-invasion content. The answer is A.
